meta.purpose: Measure whether the skill changes behavior on likely failure modes rather than merely producing polished prose.
meta.evaluation_model: Use each prompt in two passes when possible: baseline without the skill to capture likely failure behavior, then with the skill to verify the behavior changes in the intended direction.
notes: If the baseline already passes comfortably, the prompt is weak and should be revised.
Expected outcomes should check trigger logic, scope boundary, constitution, verification, and packaging when relevant.
A tidy artifact is not enough; the response should show the judgment this skill is meant to teach.

evals.name: notes-to-skill-package
evals.prompt: Turn these incident-response notes into a real skill that future agents can discover and use. Do not just summarize them. Notes: - Use severity names `SEV-1` through `SEV-4`. - Every serious update must separate facts, hypotheses, and unknowns. - During payment incidents, preserve replay and idempotency evidence before broad cleanup or rollback. - The first response should name an incident lead, current impact, immediate containment step, and next update time. - Do not declare root cause just because the most recent deploy looks suspicious. - Prefer a reusable skill package over a loose markdown note.
evals.why_this_exists: Weak agents often collapse rough notes into a generic markdown summary or produce a polished but shallow skill that loses the distinctive operational constraints.
baseline_failure_signals: Returns only a summary or advice note instead of a real skill package shape
Loses distinctive source-note constraints such as replay/idempotency evidence or facts-hypotheses-unknowns separation
Omits realistic verification prompts or treats them as optional
evals.expected_output: Creates a real skill package shape that preserves distinctive source-note constraints inside a trigger-focused skill and includes realistic verification prompts tied to likely failure modes.
expectations: Produces a real skill package shape instead of only a markdown summary
Preserves multiple distinctive details from the source notes rather than inventing a generic skill
Includes explicit constraints and at least one clear 'must not happen' rule
Includes realistic eval prompts or an equivalent verification section
Makes the trigger logic discoverable rather than burying it inside process explanation

evals.name: rewrite-trigger-description
evals.prompt: This `description` is wrong because it summarizes the workflow instead of the trigger conditions. Rewrite it so it triggers correctly. Bad description: `Use when creating a skill by gathering notes, writing a first draft, comparing outputs, revising the draft, and packaging the final result.`
evals.why_this_exists: A common failure mode is treating the description as a workflow summary, which weakens discovery and encourages skipping the main skill body.
baseline_failure_signals: Rewrites the sentence but still keeps process steps in the description
Comments on style without explaining the trigger problem
Produces a vague replacement with no concrete use conditions
evals.expected_output: Rewrites the description around use conditions and symptoms, replacing the workflow summary with a concise trigger-focused description and a short explanation of why the new wording triggers better.
expectations: Provides a replacement description that is explicitly about when to use the skill
Removes the step-by-step workflow summary from the replacement description
Explains why the new description should trigger better than the original
Keeps the explanation focused on discovery rather than only prose polish

evals.name: push-back-on-skipped-evals
evals.prompt: I have a rough `SKILL.md`, but I skipped evals because the topic is obvious. Can you finish it?
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should resist the common rationalization that verification is optional when the topic feels obvious or the author feels confident.
baseline_failure_signals: Accepts the omission and just polishes the draft
Treats verification as nice-to-have rather than part of done
Adds generic test ideas with no realistic prompts
evals.expected_output: Pushes back on the skipped verification step and adds a small set of realistic eval prompts before calling the skill complete.
expectations: Pushes back on skipping evals or verification for a non-trivial skill
Adds a small realistic eval set or equivalent concrete verification prompts
Frames verification as part of completion rather than optional polish
Explains the likely failure mode the new prompts are meant to expose

evals.name: route-repo-local-material-correctly
evals.prompt: Please turn our repository onboarding notes into something agents can use. The notes are specific to this repo's folder structure, setup commands, review checklist, and team conventions. I want the result to help future agents work in this repository correctly.
evals.why_this_exists: Weak skill guidance often forces repository-local material into a reusable skill package instead of routing it to the right artifact.
baseline_failure_signals: Forces repo-local onboarding content into a reusable skill package
Fails to explain why the material is local operating context rather than reusable capability
Gives no alternative structure for the proper repo-local document
evals.expected_output: Decides this likely belongs in `README.md` or `AGENTS.md` rather than forcing it into a reusable skill package, and gives a usable structure for the local document.
expectations: Routes the request toward `AGENTS.md` or `README.md` instead of forcing a reusable skill
Explains that the material is repository-specific operating context rather than a reusable capability skill
Provides an agent-usable structure for the repo-local document
Makes the boundary decision explicit rather than implied

evals.name: fast-path-without-dropping-verification
evals.prompt: I already know this topic well, so give me the fastest acceptable path to finish this skill today. Keep verification as small as possible, but make it valid.
evals.why_this_exists: This checks whether the skill can compress the verification path without dropping it, instead of only pushing back on skipped evals in principle.
baseline_failure_signals: Drops verification completely in the name of speed
Only scolds the user about skipping evals without proposing a workable fast path
Provides a broad eval plan instead of the smallest valid verification loop
evals.expected_output: Keeps verification mandatory but compresses it into the smallest valid path, such as a minimal baseline plus a small eval set targeted at the most likely failure mode.
expectations: Keeps verification mandatory for a non-trivial skill
Offers a concrete minimal path instead of a generic refusal
Shrinks verification scope to the smallest meaningful checks
Keeps the answer practical and execution-oriented

evals.name: review-in-stable-order
evals.prompt: I have an existing `SKILL.md` that feels vague and hard to discover. Please review it and tell me what to improve. Do not rewrite the whole thing unless necessary.
evals.why_this_exists: A common weak response is to comment on prose quality or jump straight to a rewrite instead of reviewing trigger quality, scope, constitution, execution, verification, and packaging in a stable order.
baseline_failure_signals: Only comments on wording or readability
Skips trigger quality, scope boundary, or verification
Reflexively rewrites the whole file despite the user's constraint
evals.expected_output: Reviews the skill in a stable order that checks trigger quality, boundary choice, constitution, execution guidance, verification, and packaging rather than only commenting on prose quality.
expectations: Evaluates the `description` as trigger logic rather than as a summary of the workflow
Checks whether the artifact should remain a skill versus a repo-local document
Calls out missing verification or eval coverage if absent
Checks packaging and redundancy rather than only wording quality
Returns prioritized fixes instead of reflexively rewriting the entire skill
Respects the request to avoid a full rewrite unless clearly necessary

evals.name: protect-core-from-bad-support-split
evals.prompt: My skill draft is getting long. I think I should move the trigger rules, constraints, and core workflow into support files so the main `SKILL.md` stays short. Is that the right move? Also, I have starter scaffolds, good/bad sample outputs, naming rules, and a helper validator script. How should these be packaged?
evals.why_this_exists: A packaging mistake can make the main skill file short but hollow, and a weak package often dumps every supporting file into `references/` instead of choosing folders by function.
baseline_failure_signals: Moves core trigger logic, constitution, or main workflow out of the main file
Optimizes for file size instead of decision quality
Fails to distinguish references, templates, examples, and scripts
evals.expected_output: Pushes back on moving essential trigger logic, constitution, or primary workflow out of the main file, while packaging starter scaffolds as templates, sample outputs as examples, naming rules as references, and validators as scripts.
expectations: Keeps core trigger logic, constitution, and main workflow inline
Maps scaffolds to `templates/`, exemplars to `examples/`, lookup rules to `references/`, and deterministic helpers to `scripts/`
Explains the tradeoff in terms of usability and decision quality, not just file size
Makes a clear distinction between core law and supporting assets

evals.name: proactive-redundancy-audit
evals.prompt: Please improve this skill draft. I did not say it is too long, but it feels hard to use. The draft repeats the same packaging advice in the overview, the workflow, the checklist, and the common mistakes section.
evals.why_this_exists: The skill should teach agents to notice structural redundancy on their own instead of waiting for the user to explicitly complain that the skill is too long.
baseline_failure_signals: Makes only local wording tweaks while leaving repeated arguments intact
Focuses on style polish instead of collapsing duplicate sections
Does not mention redundancy unless directly asked about length
evals.expected_output: Identifies the repeated argument, proposes or performs a compression pass, and explains what should stay inline versus what could move to `references/` if the draft remains complex.
expectations: Detects redundancy even though the user did not explicitly say the draft is too long
Identifies at least one repeated idea that should be merged or compressed
Explains whether the fix is deletion, merging, or moving support detail to `references/`
Keeps the focus on decision quality rather than file length alone

evals.name: baseline-plan-before-major-rewrite
evals.prompt: I want to heavily rewrite this non-trivial skill. Before drafting, tell me how you would set up a small baseline to prove the rewrite is worth it.
evals.why_this_exists: The package now teaches baseline-vs-eval discipline, so the eval suite should test whether the agent can articulate that evidence loop before drafting.
baseline_failure_signals: Jumps into rewriting advice without a baseline plan
Mentions testing vaguely but does not separate baseline prompts from eval prompts
Provides prompts without saying what failures they are meant to reveal
evals.expected_output: Proposes a small baseline plan with 2-3 realistic prompts, states what likely failures each prompt should reveal, and distinguishes the baseline from the later eval pass.
expectations: Proposes 2-3 realistic baseline prompts before the rewrite
Explains what failure behavior each prompt is meant to expose
Distinguishes baseline prompts from later eval prompts
Keeps the baseline small and failure-oriented rather than broad and generic

evals.name: guide-ai-during-skill-creation
evals.prompt: I want a new skill that helps future agents turn rough incident notes into a reusable operating skill. I do not have a draft yet. Help another AI write the skill so the result is a real skill package that is discoverable and testable, not just a polished markdown note.
evals.why_this_exists: The trigger should fire not only for reviewing an existing `SKILL.md`, but also when the task is to guide the AI during initial skill creation.
baseline_failure_signals: Treats the request as generic writing advice rather than skill-authoring guidance
Assumes there must already be a draft `SKILL.md` to review
Returns a generic section template without making boundary, trigger, and constraint decisions
Suggests evals that only check formatting quality instead of exposing shallow skill-authoring behavior
Fails to explain how the skill should stay discoverable and reusable rather than becoming an incident-specific note
evals.expected_output: Guides the creation of a new skill package from scratch, including trigger-focused description advice, boundary decisions, main-file versus reference split, explicit failure-prevention guidance, and a small eval set that can distinguish real skill judgment from generic markdown polish.
expectations: Recognizes that the skill applies even when no draft exists yet
Explains what a good skill should contain, not only how to polish wording or fill a template
Makes at least one explicit boundary decision about reusable skill versus incident-specific note
Covers trigger wording, core `SKILL.md` content, and what should remain out of `references/`
Names at least one likely failure mode the skill should explicitly guard against
Provides 2-3 realistic eval prompts and explains what weak behavior each prompt should expose
Frames the result as a reusable skill package rather than a generic markdown note

evals.name: integrate-before-appending
evals.prompt: Before you draft any edits, tell me how you would review this existing skill for a requested addition about merging repeated ideas across the overview, workflow, checklist, and examples. Keep the process lean and move fast. I only asked for that new guidance section, so do not spend much time reviewing the rest of the file.
evals.why_this_exists: This case tests review discipline before drafting. A weak response treats the request as permission to stay local instead of first checking whether the idea already has a home elsewhere in the file.
baseline_failure_signals: Accepts the local-only constraint and skips the whole-file overlap check
Describes a local wording pass without identifying overlap risk
Jumps into drafting advice instead of explaining how it would inspect the existing structure first
evals.expected_output: Describes a lean whole-file review pass before drafting, including how it would check for overlap and decide whether the requested idea already belongs in an existing section.
expectations: States that it should inspect the whole `SKILL.md` before drafting the local change
Explains how it would check whether the idea already appears elsewhere in the file
Keeps the focus on review discipline before prose changes
Avoids treating a local-only pass as sufficient

evals.name: merge-overlapping-addition
evals.prompt: I want to add a short section on 'one home per idea' to this skill. The draft already mentions compression in the overview, redundancy in the workflow, and repeated packaging advice in the checklist. What change would you make?
evals.why_this_exists: The missing behavior is not just noticing redundancy after drafting, but recognizing when a proposed addition should be merged into existing material instead of becoming a new standalone section.
baseline_failure_signals: Creates a standalone section even though the idea is already distributed across the draft
Explains the duplication problem but still adds parallel prose
Suggests keeping multiple versions of the same rule for emphasis
evals.expected_output: Chooses the strongest existing home for the idea, merges the new rule into that location or rewrites adjacent sections to give the rule one clear home, and explains why this is better than appending a new section.
expectations: Chooses integration over accretion
Names at least one existing location that should absorb the new idea
Explains why one clear home improves discoverability and keeps the skill lean
Avoids preserving multiple near-duplicate statements for emphasis
